1: Isaiah 17:1 opens the oracle against Damascus: 'Behold, Damascus is about to cease being a city; it will become a pile of rubble' (hineh Damascus mussar me'ir vehayethah me'i medurah, behold Damascus will be removed from being a city and will become a heap of rubble). The oracle connects to the Syro-Ephraimite crisis of 735-732 BCE — Damascus was the capital of the Aramean (Syrian) kingdom under Rezin, who allied with Pekah of Israel against Judah (Isaiah 7:1-6). Tiglath-Pileser III destroyed Damascus in 732 BCE (2 Kings 16:9: 'The king of Assyria attacked Damascus and captured it; he deported its inhabitants to Kir and put Rezin to death'). The oracle is thus accurately predictive from Isaiah's perspective, or contemporaneous with the event if composed during the 732 BCE campaign.
2: 'The towns of Aroer are abandoned — left for flocks to graze, and they will lie down there with no one to disturb them' — the depopulation imagery: the towns of Aroer (Aro'er, a town on the Arnon River in Moab/Ammon — or possibly a town in the Damascus/Syria region) abandoned and left for flocks (la'adarim, for herds). Pastoral nomads and their flocks occupying the ruins of formerly prosperous cities is the standard desolation image (Isaiah 13:20; Isaiah 34:13-15). 'No one to disturb them' (ein macharid, no one making them afraid) echoes Micah 4:4 ('every man will sit under his own vine... with no one to make them afraid') — the eschatological peace inverted as the peace of abandonment rather than fruitful security.
3: 'The fortified city will disappear from Ephraim and the royal power from Damascus; and what remains of Syria will share the same fate as the diminished glory of the Israelites, declares the LORD of hosts' — the allied powers judged together: Ephraim (the Northern Kingdom, ally of Damascus) loses its fortified cities (mibtsar meEfraim, the stronghold from Ephraim), Damascus loses its royal sovereignty (memamlekhet miDammaseq, the kingdom from Damascus). The remnant of Syria (she'ar Aram, what is left of Syria) will be 'like the glory of the children of Israel' — the same fate, the same diminishment. The Syro-Ephraimite alliance that threatened Judah in 735-732 BCE falls together. 2 Kings 15:29 records Tiglath-Pileser's deportation of northern Israelites, and 2 Kings 16:9 records Damascus's fall.
4: 'In that day the splendour of Jacob will fade and his well-nourished frame will waste away' — Jacob (the Northern Kingdom/Israel) described through two images of prosperity eroded: kevod Ya'aqov yidal (the glory/weight of Jacob will be made thin/lean) and mishman besaro yiraze (the fatness of his flesh will grow lean). The 'well-nourished frame' (mishman basar, the fat/richness of his flesh) — the prosperity of the Northern Kingdom under Jeroboam II (784-753 BCE) was exceptional (Amos 6:4-6 describes the luxury of the Samarian elite: beds of ivory, choice lambs, fine wine, the best oils). This prosperity is what 'wastes away' under divine judgment. The lean-and-thin language is the antithesis of the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 32:15 ('Jeshurun grew fat and kicked — fat and bloated and gorged').
5: 'It will be like a reaper sweeping in the grain, gathering up the ears with his arm — like someone gleaning what is left in the Valley of Rephaim' — the gleaning imagery for what remains of Israel after judgment: 'like the reaping of standing grain' (ke'asof qatsir), gathering the ears with one arm while the other cuts. The Valley of Rephaim (emeq Refa'im) was the fertile grain-producing valley southwest of Jerusalem (2 Samuel 5:18; Isaiah 23:13), famous for its harvests. The comparison: the judgment of Israel leaves only 'gleanings' (like a harvested field with a few stalks left). The gleaning theology connects to Ruth 2:2-7 (the gleaning laws of Leviticus 19:9-10 provide for the poor from the edges of harvested fields) — what remains is the deliberate remnant left for the vulnerable.
6: 'Only gleanings will remain, as when an olive tree is beaten — two or three olives at the very top of the highest branch, four or five on the outlying boughs — declares the LORD, the God of Israel' — the olive-beating image specifies the remnant's smallness: when an olive tree is beaten (ke'nofesh zayit, like the beating/threshing of an olive tree) a few olives remain at the top (rosh amir, the top of the branch) — two or three — and 'four or five on its productive boughs' (bisfiyoteha poreiyah, on its fruitful branches). The numbers (2-3, 4-5) emphasize smallness without specifying exact numbers — a 'very few.' This is the remnant theology in its most concrete form: not a comfortable minority but a barely-surviving handful. The 'YHWH God of Israel' title at the verse's end (ne'um Yhwh Elohei Yisrael) is the one who both brings the judgment and preserves the tiny remnant.
7: 'In that day people will look to their Creator, and their eyes will be fixed on the Holy One of Israel' — the theological reversal produced by judgment: 'yisheh ha'adam al-osohu, in that day the man/people will look to his Creator' (yisheh = direct attention/gaze to; osohu = his maker, the one who made him). The judgment that strips away the prosperity and military alliances also strips away the religious substitutes, leaving only the gaze toward YHWH. 'Holy One of Israel' (Qedosh Yisrael) is Isaiah's signature divine title (26 occurrences in Isaiah). The verse functions as the positive interior of the judgment: the outcome of stripping is not annihilation but restored vertical focus. Hosea 2:14-16 expresses the same theology: YHWH strips Israel of her lovers so that she will call him 'my husband' again.
8: 'He will have no regard for the altars he made, or for the Asherim poles or incense altars that his own hands fashioned' — the religious reform that judgment produces: 'he will not look toward the altars' (lo yisheh el-hamizbekhot, he will not look toward the altars) — specifically 'the altars he made' (ma'aseh yadaiv, the work of his hands). The Asherim (asheirim, plural of Asherah) are the wooden poles or sacred trees associated with the Canaanite goddess Asherah, consort of El in Canaanite religion and frequently mentioned as a cultic object in Israelite worship until Josiah's reform (2 Kings 23:4, 6, 14-15). The chammanim (incense altars, sun-pillars) were aromatic incense platforms. All these hand-made religious objects are abandoned as the 'look to the Creator' begins. Isaiah 2:20 uses the same vocabulary of discarding idols when YHWH arises in judgment.
9: 'In that day their fortified cities will be like abandoned woodland shrines and deserted hilltops that were left vacant before the Israelites — utter desolation' — the desolation of Israel's fortified cities (mivtsar) compared to the abandoned pre-Israelite Canaanite shrines (ma'aseh hachoresh vehaamir, the work of the woodland/thicket and the summit) that the Israelites found when they entered the land (lifnei venei Yisrael, before the children of Israel — meaning left by the earlier inhabitants). The comparison is precise: as the Canaanites abandoned their cultic sites when Israel arrived, so Israel's own fortified cities will be abandoned under divine judgment. The cycle of dispossession that began with the Canaanites now encompasses unfaithful Israel.
10: 'For you have forgotten the God who saves you and have not remembered the Rock who shelters you; so you plant ornamental gardens and set in shoots from foreign vines' — the theological cause of the judgment: shakhacht Elohei yish'ech (you have forgotten the God of your salvation) and tsur ma'uzekh lo zachart (the Rock of your refuge you have not remembered). The 'God of salvation' and 'Rock of refuge' are covenant titles from the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31) and the Psalms (Psalms 18:2; Isaiah 28:1; Isaiah 31:2-3; Isaiah 62:7). The response to forgetting God: planting 'ornamental gardens' (na'amanim, pleasant/beautiful gardens) and 'foreign vine shoots' (zemoroth zar, cuttings of a strange-god/alien vine). These may be pagan fertility gardens (the 'gardens of Adonis' — forcing plants in shallow soil that bloom and die quickly — known in the Hellenistic period but possibly earlier) or agricultural idolatry linked to Canaanite fertility religion.
11: 'Though you force them to sprout the very day you plant them and blossom the morning you sow — yet come the day of grief and desperate pain the harvest will be gone' — the failure of forced agricultural idolatry: 'beyom natekh tegadali uvevoqer zar'ekh tafrihi, on the day you plant them you will make them grow, and in the morning when you sow they will sprout' — the human attempt to force immediate results (suggesting magical/cultic agricultural practices designed to compel divine blessing). But 'the harvest will be gone' (nad qatsir beyom nachalah uke'ev anush, the harvest flees on the day of grief and desperate pain). The forced bloom that cannot sustain — the idolatrous quick-fix that produces no lasting harvest. The 'day of desperate pain' (uke'ev anush, and incurable pain) when the harvest fails is the judgment day that the artificial gardens cannot avert.
12: 'Woe — the uproar of many peoples! They roar like the roaring of the seas! And the crashing of nations — they crash like the surge of mighty waters!' — the cosmic sound-image for the nations' advance: 'hoy hamon ammim rabbim, woe — the sound of many peoples who sound/roar like the roaring of the seas (kehamon yammim yehemayun) and the crashing of leummim (national groups) who crash like the crashing of mighty waters (kemishbar mayim kabirim yishsaun).' The sea-roar as military advance image: the unstoppable, consuming force of the enemy army rendered as the sound of ocean surf. The nations' 'uproar' (hamon) is both the sound and the mass of the force. Isaiah uses the sea-roar image for the Assyrian advance in Isaiah 8:7-8 (the rising River Euphrates = Assyria's overflow).
13: 'The nations surge like surging water — but he rebukes them and they scatter far away, driven like chaff off the mountains before the wind, like a dust cloud spinning away before the gale' — the sudden reversal by divine rebuke: 'umim keshon mayim rabbim yishsaun, but the peoples will roar like surging mighty waters — vegaar bo veneas memerchaq (but he will rebuke it and it will flee far away).' The divine rebuke (ga'ar, the sound/word of divine authority — Psalms 18:15: 'at your rebuke, O LORD, at the blast of the breath of your nostrils') instantly disperses what seemed like an irresistible force. The two similes for the instant scattering: chaff before the wind on the mountains (kemo motz harim lifnei-ruach, like chaff of the mountains before wind) and a dust-cloud/tumbleweed before a storm (ukegalgal lifnei sufah, like a wheel/tumbleweed before a storm).
14: 'Evening comes — sudden terror. Before morning, there is nothing left. This is the destiny of those who loot us, the fate reserved for those who plunder us' — the 'evening terror, morning nothing' time-sequence: 'le'et erev vehinneh balahah, toward evening and behold — sudden terror; before morning they are gone (ainen).' The historical referent: the angel of YHWH striking 185,000 Assyrian troops in the night of Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem (2 Kings 19:35; Isaiah 37:36). The Assyrian camp full and threatening at evening; by morning 'all of them were dead bodies.' The verse 14b is a community formula: 'this is the portion of those who despoil us and the lot of those who plunder us' (ze chelek shoseinu vegoral levezeinu) — affirming the theological pattern applies to all who threaten YHWH's people. Psalms 46:5 expresses the same theology: 'God will help her at the break of dawn.'
